title: Fast React Pizza Application Planning

learningObjectives.text: Explain requirements using a new example and justify the result.
Explain feature breakdown using a new example and justify the result.
Apply routing and state decisions using a new example and justify the result.

whyThisMatters: This topic turns fast react pizza application planning from isolated terminology into a practical tool for reading, writing, and reviewing code. It belongs to the **Professional React Workflows and Ecosystem** module.
mentalModel: Draw a decision map: **Requirements** → **Feature breakdown** → **Routing and state decisions**. Add branches where the learner must choose an alternative and label the evidence for the choice.

sections.title: Requirements
sections.body: Start with user-visible outcomes and convert them into explicit behavior, inputs, edge cases, and acceptance conditions. Break large capabilities into end-to-end slices that can be implemented and verified independently.

sections.title: Feature breakdown
sections.body: Start with user-visible outcomes and convert them into explicit behavior, inputs, edge cases, and acceptance conditions. Break large capabilities into end-to-end slices that can be implemented and verified independently.

sections.title: Routing and state decisions
sections.body: **Routing and state decisions** is a core part of Fast React Pizza Application Planning. Define it by the responsibility it owns, the inputs it receives, and the observable result it produces. In React, use the concept deliberately rather than as a memorized label. Compare nearby alternatives, identify the boundary where it applies, and verify the model with a small example.

commonMistakes: Mutating props or state instead of producing the next value.
Adding state or an Effect for a value that can be derived during render.
Using global state before identifying the real owner and consumers.
Optimizing renders without a measured user-facing problem.
summary: Fast React Pizza Application Planning connects Requirements, Feature breakdown, Routing and state decisions.
Use the topic to make behavior and decisions explicit rather than memorizing isolated syntax.
Verify understanding by explaining a new example and answering the topic quiz without looking back.

topicQuiz.failurePolicy: Show explanations, add missed objectives to local review, and allow immediate or later retry.

questions.prompt: A team is making a decision involving Fast React Pizza Application Planning. Which approach shows the strongest understanding of **Requirements**?

choices.label: State the requirement and assumptions, apply the Requirements model, and verify the observable consequence before adding complexity.
choices.feedback: Transferable understanding of Requirements means using the model to make and verify a decision in a new context.

choices.label: Use the term Requirements in documentation without changing any decision or behavior.

questions.prompt: Which statement best distinguishes **Requirements** from **Feature breakdown** in this topic?

choices.label: They address different parts of the topic's model; a correct decision identifies which one explains the current requirement and how the two interact.
choices.feedback: The concepts are related but should be applied according to the requirement and the role each plays in Fast React Pizza Application Planning.

choices.label: They are interchangeable labels, so choosing between them has no effect on reasoning.

questions.hint: Look for a distinction based on responsibilities or consequences, not an absolute rule.

questions.prompt: A developer notices this recurring mistake: “Mutating props or state instead of producing the next value.” What is the best response?

choices.label: Return to the requirement, use the Fast React Pizza Application Planning model to identify the failed assumption, and verify the corrected behavior with a representative case.
choices.feedback: The useful response corrects both the misconception and the next development action for Fast React Pizza Application Planning.

choices.label: Hide the symptom and continue so the schedule is not affected.
choices.feedback: This choice does not resolve the assessed issue. Re-trace the Fast React Pizza Application Planning rule and the observable result.

choices.label: Add unrelated abstractions before reproducing the problem.
choices.feedback: This choice does not resolve the assessed issue. Re-trace the Fast React Pizza Application Planning rule and the observable result.

choices.label: Treat the mistake as unavoidable and remove the relevant check.
choices.feedback: This choice does not resolve the assessed issue. Re-trace the Fast React Pizza Application Planning rule and the observable result.

questions.explanation: The useful response corrects both the misconception and the next development action for Fast React Pizza Application Planning.
questions.hint: Choose the response that makes the failure reproducible and the correction verifiable.

questions.prompt: Which day-to-day workflow is most reliable when a task depends on Fast React Pizza Application Planning?

choices.label: Clarify the requirement, identify the relevant model or boundary, test the smallest representative case, then refactor after the behavior is understood.
choices.feedback: A reliable workflow makes assumptions and failures visible early, then improves the implementation after correctness is established.

choices.label: Start with the largest available abstraction and postpone defining expected behavior.
choices.feedback: This choice does not resolve the assessed issue. Re-trace the Fast React Pizza Application Planning rule and the observable result.

choices.label: Copy a solution from another project without checking whether its assumptions match.
choices.feedback: This choice does not resolve the assessed issue. Re-trace the Fast React Pizza Application Planning rule and the observable result.

choices.label: Optimize for line count first and inspect correctness only after release.
choices.feedback: This choice does not resolve the assessed issue. Re-trace the Fast React Pizza Application Planning rule and the observable result.

questions.explanation: A reliable workflow makes assumptions and failures visible early, then improves the implementation after correctness is established.
questions.hint: Prefer the sequence that produces evidence early and keeps decisions reversible.

questions.prompt: Which guideline shows the best engineering judgment about Fast React Pizza Application Planning?

choices.label: Use Fast React Pizza Application Planning to reduce uncertainty: document the requirement, choose a proportionate approach, and leave evidence that another developer can review.
choices.feedback: Use Fast React Pizza Application Planning to reduce uncertainty: document the requirement, choose a proportionate approach, and leave evidence that another developer can review.

choices.label: Use Fast React Pizza Application Planning in every file because more abstraction always improves software.
choices.feedback: This choice does not resolve the assessed issue. Re-trace the Fast React Pizza Application Planning rule and the observable result.

choices.label: Avoid Fast React Pizza Application Planning completely because every additional concept reduces maintainability.
choices.feedback: This choice does not resolve the assessed issue. Re-trace the Fast React Pizza Application Planning rule and the observable result.

choices.label: Choose only by the number of lines and ignore correctness, ownership, and future change.
choices.feedback: This choice does not resolve the assessed issue. Re-trace the Fast React Pizza Application Planning rule and the observable result.

questions.explanation: Use Fast React Pizza Application Planning to reduce uncertainty: document the requirement, choose a proportionate approach, and leave evidence that another developer can review.
questions.hint: Choose the option that balances correctness, clarity, cost, and the actual requirement.

researchSources.title: Application Planning
researchSources.usage: Coverage research only; no transcript wording is canonical.

contentAuthority.canonical: New JS2Next Release 1 curriculum and independently authored content.
contentAuthority.legacyProjectContent: Supplementary internal reference only; excluded from runtime unless explicitly re-authored and approved.
contentAuthority.courseTranscripts: Coverage and sequencing research only; never learner-facing authority.

assessmentProfile.label: Developer workflow and decision scenarios
assessmentProfile.rationale: The learner should make realistic development decisions using requirements, logs, file structures, configuration, or short code artifacts.
assessmentProfile.inLessonPolicy: Use concise concept checks; coding and hybrid topics include at least one code-reading check.
assessmentProfile.topicQuizPolicy: Questions Q04–Q08 form the topic quiz. The presentation mix follows this profile.
